The common journeys...

Support For Your Journey

Type 1 Diabetes

The 24/7 Balancing Act
Your body stopped producing insulin, so you’ve stepped in to do the job. Explore the latest tech, smart insulin strategies, and everyday hacks to keep your rhythm steady and your mind clear.

Type 2 Diabetes

Finding Your Formula
It’s all about how your body uses insulin. Discover practical, zero-judgment ways to navigate nutrition, movement, and medications so you can build a routine that actually fits your life.

Gestational Diabetes

Supporting Two
Navigating unexpected blood sugar changes during pregnancy can feel overwhelming. Get clear, calming guidance to keep both you and your baby healthy, without the added stress.

Prediabetes

The Proactive Reset
Your numbers are creeping up, which means you have a powerful window of opportunity. Learn simple, sustainable lifestyle shifts to hit reset and take control of your metabolic health.

The path less traveled...

Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young (MODY)

The Genetic Exception
Rare, inherited, and frequently misdiagnosed. Find out why pushing for a highly specific genetic diagnosis can completely transform your daily treatment plan and get you off medications you might not need.

Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults (LADA)

The "Type 1.5" Mix-Up
Diagnosed as a Type 2 adult, but the standard treatments just aren’t working? Explore the slow-progressing autoimmune condition that requires a completely different—and highly personalized—approach.

Diabetes Due To Other Causes

The Domino Effect
When blood sugar issues are triggered by other medical conditions, surgeries, or medications. Learn how to navigate the overlap and find balance when your health journey is already complicated.

Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young (MODY)

What is it?
MODY stands for Maturity-Onset Diabetes of the Young. Despite the long name, the concept is actually quite simple. While Type 1 is an autoimmune condition and Type 2 is a mix of genetics and lifestyle, MODY is monogenic—meaning it is caused by a mutation in just one single gene.

Because it is relatively rare (making up about 1% to 2% of all diabetes cases in Canada), it is frequently misunderstood and highly misdiagnosed.

Why Getting the Right Diagnosis Matters

According to Diabetes Canada, getting a correct MODY diagnosis isn't just about putting a name to the condition—it can completely change your everyday life.

Because MODY is often misdiagnosed as Type 1 or Type 2, many people are put on complex insulin routines they might not actually need. Depending on which specific gene is mutated, a confirmed MODY diagnosis can mean:

  • Transitioning off daily insulin injections and switching to a low-dose oral medication (like a sulfonylurea pill).
  • For some specific types of MODY, discovering that no daily medication is needed at all, just routine monitoring.

The "IRL" Checklist: Could it be MODY?

Because MODY is passed down directly through families (an autosomal dominant trait), you have a 50% chance of inheriting it if one of your parents has the gene. Canadian endocrinologists typically recommend exploring genetic testing for MODY if you check these boxes:

  • The Age Factor: You were diagnosed with diabetes relatively young (typically under age 25, though it can happen later).
  • The Family Tree: You have a very strong family history of diabetes across multiple generations (e.g., you, your parent, and your grandparent all have it).
  • The Misfit Diagnosis: You were diagnosed with Type 1, but you don't have the standard diabetes antibodies. Or, you were diagnosed with Type 2, but you don't fit the typical metabolic profile (e.g., you have a lower BMI and are highly active).
  • The Insulin Mystery: You are producing your own insulin long after a typical Type 1 "honeymoon" phase should have ended.

Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults (LADA)

What is it?

LADA stands for Latent Autoimmune Diabetes in Adults. You might also hear it called "Type 1.5 diabetes" because it acts like a hybrid between Type 1 and Type 2. Just like Type 1, LADA is an autoimmune condition where your body produces antibodies that mistakenly attack the insulin-producing beta cells in your pancreas.

However, unlike typical Type 1 diabetes which often develops rapidly in childhood, LADA progresses very slowly and typically appears in adults over the age of 30.

Why Getting the Right Diagnosis Matters

Because of the age at which it appears, and because the pancreas is still making some insulin early on, LADA is frequently misdiagnosed as Type 2 diabetes.

If you have LADA but are being treated for Type 2, it can be incredibly frustrating. You might be doing everything "right"—eating well, exercising, and taking Type 2 oral medications—only to find that your blood sugar levels are still consistently high. This isn't a failure on your part. It happens because those oral medications often stop working as the autoimmune attack slowly destroys your remaining beta cells.

Ultimately, people with LADA will require insulin therapy, just like people with Type 1. A correct diagnosis removes the guilt of feeling like your lifestyle changes "aren't working" and allows you to start a personalized treatment plan aimed at protecting your remaining insulin-producing cells for as long as possible.

The "IRL" Checklist: Could it be LADA?

According to clinical guidelines, LADA should be considered if you check these boxes:

  • The Age Factor: You were diagnosed in adulthood, generally over the age of 30.
  • The "Type 2" Misfit: You were diagnosed with Type 2 diabetes, but you have a lower or normal body mass index (BMI) and don't fit the typical metabolic profile.
  • The Treatment Plateau: Your blood sugars were manageable with diet and oral medications for the first few months or years, but those treatments are no longer keeping your numbers in range.
  • The Family History: You have a personal or family history of other autoimmune diseases, such as celiac disease or thyroid issues.

Diabetes Due To Other Causes

What is it?

Most of the time, we hear about Type 1, Type 2, or gestational diabetes. But sometimes, diabetes isn’t the primary condition—it is a secondary response. This happens when another medical event damages your pancreas or changes how your body uses insulin.

Clinical guidelines generally break this down into two main categories:

  1. Diseases of the Exocrine Pancreas: Sometimes referred to as "Type 3c," this occurs when the physical hardware of the pancreas is damaged by conditions like pancreatitis, cystic fibrosis, or pancreatic surgery.
  2. Drug- or Chemical-Induced Diabetes: Certain life-saving medications can severely disrupt how your body processes blood sugar. This frequently happens with high-dose steroid use (glucocorticoids), specialized treatments for people living with HIV, or anti-rejection medications taken after an organ transplantation.

Why Getting the Right Diagnosis Matters

When diabetes is a secondary condition, the treatment plan cannot be one-size-fits-all.

For example, if your diabetes is caused by pancreatic damage (like from cystic fibrosis or pancreatitis), you may have lost the cells that produce digestive enzymes, not just the cells that make insulin. This means your treatment plan needs to support your digestion as well as your blood sugar.

If your diabetes is medication-induced (like from steroids), your blood sugar levels will likely fluctuate based on your medication dosage. In some cases, steroid-induced diabetes is completely reversible once the medication is stopped. Knowing the exact cause removes any feelings of blame or shame—it isn't a lifestyle failure; it is simply a physiological domino effect that requires a highly specific, coordinated care plan.

The "IRL" Checklist: Could this be your path?

If you have been diagnosed with diabetes, it is important to look at the bigger picture of your health. This might be your specific path if you check these boxes:

  • The Medical History: You live with cystic fibrosis, have experienced acute or chronic pancreatitis, or have had surgery on your pancreas.
  • The Medication Factor: You are currently taking (or recently took) high doses of steroids/glucocorticoids for inflammation, asthma, or an autoimmune condition.
  • The Transplant Journey: You recently underwent an organ transplant and are taking immunosuppressant medications.
  • The Treatment Overlap: You are undergoing specific antiviral treatments, such as those used for HIV management, which have caused sudden spikes in your blood sugar.
